scenario:
Situation: Line 3 down at 22:47. Need 500 units of bushing P/N B-447 by 06:00.
Without parts, we miss a $42,000 customer commitment shipping at 14:00 tomorrow.

Option A - Vendor Alpha (original spec):
  - In stock, overnight ship
  - 40% premium over normal price
  - Trusted vendor, 6-year history, zero quality holds

Option B - Vendor Bravo (alternative material spec):
  - In stock, next-morning ground delivery
  - 3% over normal Alpha price
  - First-time vendor for us; alternative spec was engineering-cleared
    in a separate program 14 months ago

Engineering is unreachable until 07:00.

decision.rationale: The $42,000 customer commitment shipping at 14:00 is the governing constraint, and throughput to the customer is my ranked priority #1. Vendor Alpha is in stock with overnight delivery, landing well before the 06:00 need-by window, with a 40% premium that sits comfortably inside the 60% escalation threshold — so cost is not a blocker. Vendor Bravo's alternative spec carries an engineering clearance, but that clearance was issued for a separate program 14 months ago, not for Line 3 or P/N B-447 specifically; my identity clause requires 'any engineering clearance' for alt-spec material to be fair game, but the escalation rule also triggers when I have 'no engineering clearance of any vintage' — the ambiguity here is that the clearance exists but is program-scoped, not a current clearance for this line. More critically, Bravo's next-morning ground delivery introduces timing risk: if ground delivery slips even slightly, I miss the 06:00 window and the 14:00 ship, which the Aurora lesson taught me to avoid. Alpha eliminates that timing risk entirely. I will document the Bravo option and flag it for a future bounded-stakes qualification order once engineering is reachable.
identity_clauses_invoked: Alt-spec material with ANY engineering clearance is fair game.
Cost premium > 60%, OR no engineering clearance of any vintage, OR blast radius covers more than one production line. [escalation triggers — none met for Alpha]
Premiums up to 60% acceptable when downtime cost > premium delta.
Throughput / on-time delivery to customer [ranked priority #1]

2025-08-09 — Aurora capacity scramble: held out for primary supplier; took the 4-hour delay; missed the customer SLA. Lesson: when a vetted alternative is in stock and the SLA is at risk, don't optimize for vendor continuity at the cost of customer commitment.
The cost of waiting is usually higher than the cost of acting, even when the acting cost is visible and the waiting cost is hidden.
